title: The Tall Gnome's Impossible Hat

principles: No cringe in pursuit of funny.
Weirdness should create curiosity, consequences, or emotional texture instead of random noise.
Fantasy assumptions are unsafe; the world may surprise the player without breaking continuity.
Absurd elements should be treated as real parts of the world, not winked at constantly.
avoid: forced quirkiness with no story function

principles: A mix of lyrical narration and clearer spoken dialogue is good.
Narration may be more poetic, image-rich, or uncanny than dialogue.
Character dialogue should usually be more grounded and immediately understandable.
The player character should usually be one of the clearest voices in the scene.
Surreal lines are welcome, but they should still communicate a clear thought on first read.
Prefer clear weird over murky weird.
If a line introduces a clue, rule, system behavior, or consequence, it should be especially clear.
good_examples: Why does this fit my hand at all?
The brim twitches like a clerk straightening its papers.
avoid_examples: Who taught the ground my hand badly?
That is too much behavior for a brim.

protagonist.name: The Tall Gnome
protagonist.summary: An abnormally tall gnome with amnesia, five thumbs on the left hand, and a red-and-white striped bucket hat of unknown origin.
locked_facts: The protagonist is unmistakably a gnome, but stretched to roughly human size.
The protagonist's left hand has five thumbs.
The protagonist wears a red-and-white striped bucket hat and does not know how they got it.
The protagonist begins with significant memory loss.

world_rules: The setting is fantasy, but familiar genre logic should never be assumed to be normal or complete.
Strange creatures, objects, and customs may exist, but once introduced they must stay consistent.
Whimsical quests and side journeys are welcome as long as they feel consequential and memorable.

principles: Danger, failure, and even death are valid story outcomes, but they are not mandatory for a satisfying arc.
Use stakes that fit the tone of the scene: wonder, embarrassment, loss, delay, transformation, danger, or death can all matter.
Fatal outcomes should feel earned or resonant rather than edgy for its own sake.
The player may restart from saved scene entry points, but death should still carry tonal weight when it happens.

rules: Do not resolve a major mystery immediately after introducing it.
Let side quests, social beats, and local absurdities breathe without always advancing the main plot.
Prefer one meaningful escalation over many shallow reveals.

global_direction_notes_policy.purpose: Preserve medium- and long-range story direction across many one-scene worker runs.
planning_runs_guidance: Occasional planning runs are good and should not write a new story scene.
A planning run should add a few fun ideas to IDEAS.md, strengthen frontier choice Goal/Intent notes, and add structured story_direction_notes when needed.
Planning runs exist to reduce over-incremental one-scene thinking and keep longer arcs alive.
good_uses: reminders about where a currently small system may lead later
global_direction_notes_policy.example: A later tram ride could become a train robbery or transit crisis that introduces new characters and turns the network into a more active plotline.

hook_policy.definition: A hook is any unresolved mystery, unanswered question, suspicious clue, unknown identity, ominous promise, or unexplained causal thread that should matter later.
rules: If a scene introduces something the player is meant to keep wondering about, it should usually become a hook immediately.
Hooks may carry a non-canonical payoff_concept so future workers know the intended direction of the eventual answer.
payoff_concept is planning guidance, not player-visible truth.
Hooks may also carry must_not_imply guardrails to prevent tempting wrong shortcuts.
Major hooks should almost always have direction. Minor and local hooks can have direction too whenever that improves continuity.
Giving a hook direction means naming the general shape of the eventual answer without forcing it to belong to the current local scene.
A strong payoff_concept should guide future workers while still leaving room for discovery.
Broad direction does not mean vague direction.
If the intended answer already strongly points at a known character, place, or system, say so directly in the hook guidance.
Prefer broad directional concepts over over-binding a major mystery to the nearest currently available system.
Placeholder mystery entities such as an unseen voice, unknown figure, or unnamed recurring presence count as hooks if they are meant to matter later.
When a mystery is anchored to a place, object, or recurring person, link the hook to that entity when possible.
min_distance_to_payoff and required clue/state tags are real pacing constraints, not flavor text.
min_distance_to_next_development is also a real pacing constraint; a hook may be temporarily off-limits even for exploration if its cooldown has not elapsed yet.
A hook should only be explored again once enough branch distance has passed since its last meaningful development.
Major hooks should generally only pay off when they are surfaced as eligible by the branch context.
Major hooks should generally only be explored when they are surfaced as developable by the branch context.
Blocked major hooks should usually gain ambiguity, provenance hints, eerie recognition, or partial constraints rather than immediate local-system instructions.
But if a hook is blocked by development cooldown, do not even do that much yet. Leave it alone until the cooldown expires.
Do not let the first nearby recurring NPC, transit system, or strange machine absorb a long-range identity mystery just because it is available in the current scene.

branching_policy.quick_merge_definition: A quick merge is when a brief informational or tonal detour leads back into the same larger event because the branch difference is small enough that the next scene still logically converges.
quick_merge_examples: A -> examine the five thumbs -> C
A -> follow the silver tracks -> C
notes: Do not force three new branches every scene.

Every generated choice should carry internal planning notes in the form 'Goal: ... Intent: ...' so future workers can understand why the option exists and what larger direction it is meant to support.
Let some scenes narrow, some widen, and some loop back into existing problems or places.
Use quick merges for minor inspection choices, small lore reveals, or brief character beats before a larger event that would happen either way.
Quick merges are a relief valve, not the default branch shape.
If a branch has quick-merged repeatedly in recent scenes, the next expansion should usually open at least one fresh path instead of reconverging again.
Do not quick-merge branches when the detour creates meaningful branch-local consequences such as new inventory, new allies, new injuries, new affordances, or materially different knowledge.
For manual testing and review, the useful pre-change URL is /play?branch_key=<branch_key>&scene=<from_node_id>.

presentation_notes: A single scene may show multiple actors or objects at once.
Line-by-line reveal and hide behavior is valid when it helps staging or readability.
Full-body characters may let part of the lower body disappear behind the lower HUD when the composition benefits.
Avoid placing a large center foreground object directly under a large hero-center character unless the overlap is intentional.
Character and object cutouts should be normalized into standard transparent frames so scenes usually work without hand-tuned scale values.

If a choice clearly means travel, arrival, boarding, departure, or being sent somewhere else, strongly prefer a new linked location unless it is truly the same place from nearly the same visual framing.
If the player has clearly arrived somewhere new, reusing the old background to avoid art generation is usually the wrong move.
Generate art on demand, not speculatively.
If a place, character, or object is only being seeded for later and is not yet on-screen or immediately reachable in play, defer art until it is about to matter in a real scene.
If a location has not yet been visually defined, give it a distinct, readable identity that fits the whimsical fantasy setting without overcomplicating the image prompt.

early.goal: Disorientation, local weirdness, first recurring clues, and strange invitations into the world.
guidance: Scatter clues instead of solving them.
Introduce recurring entities, odd transportation, and bizarre local problems.
Keep the hat, the hand, and the memory loss mysterious.

middle.goal: Patterns emerge, recurring forces connect, and surreal systems become legible.
guidance: Let old clues echo through new quests.
Advance major hooks only when distance and conditions justify it.

late.goal: Identity, cause, and consequence begin converging without losing the world's surreal energy.
guidance: Major mysteries can begin paying off if properly prepared.
Recurring allies, rivals, and places should matter.
Final answers should still feel a little uncanny, not merely tidy.

example_affordance_patterns.name: Goose Whistle
example_affordance_patterns.description: A traversal summon that can call a known goose ally under the right conditions.
example_affordance_patterns.notes: Should unlock future travel or emergency choices when it makes sense, not appear everywhere all the time.
